costs: TEIP-style targeted funding line for disadvantaged schools — no numeric fiscal range available in published Hungarian sources, and the scenario's own political-risk field identifies this as a likely first target for fiscal-consolidation cuts, making its effective, durable cost highly uncertain despite its nominal budget line existing.
Curriculum-clarity and assessment-transparency reform costs, one-off design plus recurring implementation-support costs, magnitude unpublished.

Grade-retention reduction programme costs, offset by documented savings (Portugal's EUR 112 million over two years across 663 schools), suggesting this specific line item may be net-cost-saving rather than cost-adding if implemented at comparable scale and fidelity.
content.equity_impact: The scenario could be equity-neutral or even equity-negative net of its entrenchment effect on the status quo, because it explicitly leaves the 10-12 selection age and the associated removal of the highest-performing, often highest-SES pupils from the general-school population untouched, legitimizing early selection by demonstrating that 'something is being done' without addressing its documented segregation effect (highest in the EU) or the near-zero admission share of multiply-disadvantaged pupils in elite tracks; there is also a risk of widening the gap between low-SES high-performers (who benefit from compensatory investment but may still be excluded from elite tracks by admission gatekeeping) and low-SES low/average-performers.
expected_benefits: Achievement gains in the range of Portugal's observed 0.3 SD improvement over roughly 15-18 years are plausible for the comprehensive (non-tracked) majority of Hungarian pupils if curriculum, autonomy, and assessment reforms are implemented with comparable fidelity.

Politically less disruptive than structural reform since it does not touch the statutory definition of secondary education, avoiding the felmenő-rendszer obligations and the direct Fidesz 'totalitarian unified model' framing risk that S3 faces — though this should not be read as evidence the package is politically risk-free, given that targeted funding for disadvantaged schools is independently a fiscal-consolidation target.

Grade-retention reduction offers a documented dual fiscal-and-equity win independent of the more uncertain TEIP competency-gap question, based directly on the Portuguese EUR 112 million/two-year, 26%-reduction precedent.
content.goal: Pursue equity and achievement gains through curriculum clarity, targeted (TEIP-style) funding for disadvantaged schools, retention reduction, and school-quality levelling, without altering the 10-12 selection age or track structure.

implementation_steps.action: Design curriculum-clarity and assessment-transparency standards for general (non-tracked) schools, sequenced explicitly alongside the already-committed NAT and AI-integration reform timelines to avoid CPD-bandwidth conflict.
implementation_steps.actor: Ministry of Education / National Core Curriculum body

implementation_steps.action: Establish a TEIP-style targeted-funding mechanism with an explicit statutory (cardinal-law) funding floor rather than an annual-budget line, directly responding to the scenario's own political-risk finding that non-statutory targeted funding is a likely first-line cut; achieving statutory-floor status is itself uncertain given projected fiscal consolidation and should be treated as a contested legislative negotiation, not an administrative step, extending the realistic timeline beyond initial design.
implementation_steps.actor: Ministry of Education + Ministry of Finance

implementation_steps.action: Roll out grade-retention reduction programme at national scale, drawing on Portugal's documented model, with annual monitoring of both fiscal savings and retained-student outcomes.

Commission a longitudinal matched-cohort evaluation of TEIP-style programme effects on competency gains (not just dropout/retention), addressing the documented absence of rigorous long-term evaluation.

mechanisms: Portugal's documented gains (approximately 31 PISA points / 0.3 SD between 2000-2018) resulted from curriculum standards clarification, increased school autonomy, and regular student assessment — not from changes to selection age — providing a template mechanism transferable in principle to Hungary's comprehensive-sector (non-tracked) majority.

TEIP-style targeted funding reduces student dropout and grade retention, but does not close competency gaps relative to non-TEIP schools, and no rigorous longitudinal evaluation of its long-term student-outcome effects exists.

Grade-retention reduction alone saved Portugal EUR 112 million over two years while reducing retained students by 26% across 663 schools, demonstrating that this specific lever can be both fiscally and pedagogically favorable, independent of the TEIP funding question.

Non-structural reform can operate independently of decisions about selection age and intensity only to a limited degree, since the mechanism field itself notes that early selection may offset some benefits of compensatory investment by continuing to remove the highest-performing, often highest-SES pupils from the general-school population that this scenario's interventions target — meaning the scenario's benefits are bounded by a structural factor it does not touch.
risks: Targeted TEIP-style funding lines are likely first-line cuts during fiscal consolidation (deficit projected at 6.2% in 2026, debt rising to 76.8% of GDP by 2027), making the central benefit-delivery mechanism budget-vulnerable in a way that significantly undermines the durability of any expected equity gain, even though the scenario avoids the structural-reform reversal risks facing S1-S3.

The scenario's political attractiveness as a 'lower-risk' alternative may itself become a political liability if framed by reform advocates or the European Commission as inadequate given Hungary's documented status as the EU's most socially selective system, risking accusations of policy avoidance rather than genuine reform.

Teacher-union (PSZ) framing that positions workforce adequacy (16,000+ shortage) as the binding, non-negotiable precondition applies to this scenario's curriculum and TEIP-delivery mechanisms as much as to structural alternatives, meaning workforce constraints could stall implementation regardless of the scenario's structural modesty.
content.title: Non-structural compensatory reform (leave selection age unchanged)
